entry.title: Re: Setting Up Enterprise Organizational Management in S/4HANA Service
entry.content:
Hello,

In SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS02– Enterprise Organizational Management, the assignment of a Service Organization to a Sales Area follows a 1:1 relationship. A Service Organization can be assigned to only one Sales Organization. There are cases where there will be more than one service organization that needs to assign one sales organization.

Can someone suggest how we can assign multiple service org to single sales area.

Thanks in advance

entry.title: AI-Supported GxP Validation: How SAP and DHC Are Reimagining Compliance in the Age of Cloud
entry.content:
Welcome to this blog post! In today's edition, I want to share how life sciences companies can modernize their GxP validation processes using AI-powered tools from SAP and SAP Partner DHC Consulting - specifically the GxP Release Change Assessment Agent and the DHC Smart Validation Accelerator (SVA). This post is based on our recent Meet the Expert webinar on AI-Supported GxP Validation. No time to read? Register to access the recording of our latest Meet the Expert webinar here.

The Validation Challenge Is Getting Harder

Life sciences companies operating in GxP-regulated environments face a growing challenge: Regulations change and products are updated constantly - quarterly for major releases, with additional hotfixes and bug fixes shipped even more frequently. For SAP Digital Manufacturing, for example, the window between deployment to non-productive tenants and deployment to productive tenants is just two weeks.

That means a regulated customer has two weeks to assess the impact of up to 50 to 200 changes, determine which changes affect their validated processes, and execute the required revalidation activities - before the update goes live in production whether they are ready or not. There is no opt-out.

 

[The image illustrates the two-week revalidation window for SAP Digital Manufacturing and the quarterly/weekly release cadence that drives the urgency.]

 

So how can validation teams keep pace and be efficient?

From Documents to Digitized Processes: The V-Model Reimagined

 The starting point for efficient validation is a clear understanding of what validation actually means in this context. The V-Model - well established in the industry under the GAMP 5 framework - defines a process-based, risk-focused approach that links requirements, functional specifications, and testing activities to each other. The key insight is that validation is process-based: companies are validating their processes, not individual transactions, Fiori apps, or features in isolation.

 This matters because when a change arrives, the question is not "what changed in the software?" but "which of my validated processes are affected by this change, and what do I need to do about it?"

[The image shows how the V-Model integrates with SAP Activate phases, from requirements and functional specifications on the left to testing (IQ/OQ/PQ) on the right, with risk-based analysis at the center.]

 

The SAP End-to-End Digital Validation Platform

To make this efficient at scale, you need a connected toolchain. SAP and DHC have built an end-to-end validation platform that integrates the following tools:

 

  • SAP Cloud ALM - the central application lifecycle management platform, serving as the hub for requirements, change management, test management, and transport management.
  • Signavio - for process modeling, enabling the business-IT fit between how a company describes its processes and how they are implemented and validated in SAP.
  • LeanIX - for enterprise architecture management, mapping the application landscape across SAP and non-SAP tools that are part of validated processes.
  • DHC Smart Validation Accelerator (SVA) - an add-on to Cloud ALM that auto-generates GxP-compliant validation documentation (User Requirement Specifications, Functional Specifications, Configuration Specifications, Design Specification, Test Scripts) from the information already captured in Cloud ALM.
  • Tricentis Tosca - integrated for test automation, enabling OQ-level automated test execution, with results fed back into the validation documentation.
  • SAP GxP Release Change Assessment Agent - the AI-powered core of the impact assessment process, analyzing product code changes, release documentation, and the product service catalog to identify validation-relevant changes.

 

[The image shows the full toolchain across the SAP Activate phases from Discover to Run, linking LeanIX, Cloud ALM, DHC SVA, and Tricentis.]

 

If you want to keep up with recurring, frequent changes, you need to automate your change process: This is what an automatic change process may look like:

[The image illustrates the six-step automated change process: from change request creation through identification of validation activities, test execution, verification, transport to production, and change closure.]

 

The GxP Release Change Assessment Agent: How It Works

The heart of the new capability is the SAP GxP Release Change Assessment Agent - a Forward Deployed Engineering (FDE) project co-developed with life sciences customers including Roche, Novartis, AstraZeneca, Sartorius, and Gilead.

The agent operates through a multi-step pipeline:

1. Change Identification

All changes for a given release are extracted from the What's New viewer on the SAP Help Portal. Each change item is linked to a specific feature and capability within the product's service catalog, providing a clear mapping between "what changed" and "where it sits in the product."

 2. Customer Applicability Filtering

The agent uses the product service catalog - configured once by the customer to reflect which capabilities they have enabled and use - to filter out changes that affect features outside the customer's validated scope. Only in-scope changes proceed to the next steps.

 3. GxP Relevancy Analysis (AI-assisted)

Each in-scope change is evaluated against six GxP criteria: patient safety impact, product quality, data integrity, traceability impact, operational continuity, and regulatory compliance. The analysis is grounded in actual regulatory documents - 21 CFR Part 211, 21 CFR Part 11, EU Annex 11, ICH Q9, and ICH Q10 - not general AI knowledge. The system assigns a confidence level and a justification for each criterion. Where confidence is insufficient to make a determination, the change is flagged for mandatory human review.

 4. Risk Assessment (FMECA)

For GxP-relevant changes, an AI model performs a failure mode, effects, and criticality analysis - identifying potential failure modes, assessing severity, and calculating a Risk Priority Number (RPN) to set a risk category. This is done per change item, with full transparency and the ability for qualified persons to edit, override, or add risk entries.

 5. Action Plan Generation

A consolidated action plan is generated per feature - not per individual change - to avoid fragmented planning. The plan outlines validation actions, test scope, documentation impact, configuration verification needs, integration testing aspects, revalidation assessment, and regulatory filing impact.

 6. Export and Integration

The completed assessment is available as a PDF, Excel, or CSV export for audit purposes. Integration with Cloud ALM is in development, enabling direct hand-off to the change management and test execution workflow.

 

[The image shows a visual overview of the five-step agentic pipeline (Change Identification, Filter, GxP Analysis, Risk/FMECA, Action Plan), showing which steps are deterministic and which are AI-grounded, plus the regulatory documents used for grounding.]

The AI Impact Generator scans actual product code repositories to generate a grounded impact analysis, building dependency graphs and assessing operational continuity, data integrity, traceability, and product quality.

 

What Makes This Different: Code-Level Analysis

One aspect of this agent that is uniquely possible because it is built by SAP for SAP products is the AI Impact Generator. This component scans the actual product code repositories for each release - something only SAP as the vendor can do. It builds a dependency graph across code repositories, identifies technical metadata changes (CDS views, authorization scopes, MTA descriptors, JavaScript dependencies), and generates a system impact summary that provides additional grounding for the GxP analysis step. This is not exposed externally to customers, but it significantly improves the quality and accuracy of the agent assessments compared to what is possible from release notes alone.

 

Connecting Agent Output to Customer Validation Documentation

The GxP Release Change Assessment Agent is only one side of the equation. The other is connecting its output to the customer's own validation documentation - the user requirements, functional specifications, configuration specifications, and test scripts that exist in the customer's Cloud ALM instance.

This is where DHC's Smart Validation Accelerator closes the loop. The DHC SVA automatically generates GxP-compliant validation documentation from the information already structured in Cloud ALM - user stories, requirements, process flows. Each item is versioned, has an electronic approval workflow with electronic signature, and maintains a full audit trail.

The connection point between the two systems is the service catalog capability IDs. These IDs appear both in the What's New viewer (as the "impacted feature" attribute on each change) and in the validation documentation generated by SVA (linked to user stories and functional specifications). When the GxP Release Change Assessment Agent identifies that a specific capability was affected by a change, the SVA can trace exactly which process, requirements, functional specifications, and test cases in the customer's documentation are linked to that capability - and therefore which validation activities need to be triggered.

[The image shows the handoff between the SAP GxP Change Agent (analyzing product code changes, release documentation, and the service catalog) and the DHC SVA (managing customer validation documentation).]

 

Products Currently in Scope

The GxP Release Change Assessment Agent is currently in the productization phase and is planned for availability around November 2026. The products currently onboarded are the following:

 - SAP Digital Manufacturing

- SAP Advanced Therapy Orchestration

- SAP Virtual Research

- SAP ICSM (Intelligent Clinical Supply Management)

- SAP Batch Release Hub (in progress)

 

SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP is not yet GxP-qualified but is in the process of being qualified, with onboarding to the agent expected in a subsequent release in 2027.

 

Conclusion

The combination of the SAP GxP Release Change Assessment Agent and the DHC Smart Validation Accelerator represents a significant step forward for life sciences companies navigating the realities of cloud-native SAP products. The promise is not just automation - it is structured, auditable, traceable, and defensible validation that keeps pace with the speed of cloud delivery.

 For GxP-regulated companies that are still managing this process manually or with partial standardization, now is the time to engage. The toolchain is here. The regulatory grounding is in place. And the window between each new release keeps getting shorter.

 

More Information

If you are interested in learning more about GxP readiness, sign up for our upcoming webinars on the SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub.

Also, make sure you won’t miss the DHC Life Sciences Days, featuring SAP AI in GxP, on 16-17 September. Register via https://www.dhc-consulting.com/.

Also, find out more about SAP GxP Readiness on sap.com.

 

Don't hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts.

 

- Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up to date with our latest enablement assets: https://www.linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo

entry.title: Re: Writing (Posting) Data to Non-Leaf Members in Advance...
entry.content: I restructured my model based on previous advice by redefining the Account and Cost Center dimensions so that all members are leaf members, including those that were originally non-leaf members. To preserve the original hierarchy logic, I stored the former non-leaf members as properties (keys) and used them to expand the # values for cost breakdown classification. Current Behavior After converting everything to leaf members: Values are correctly assigned to members that were originally leaf members However, members that were originally non-leaf do not have any data physically stored In summary: Former leaf members → values exist Former non-leaf members → values do not exist Requirement I would like to understand: How can I physically store fact data for members that were originally non-leaf? Additional Assumption For my use case, I consider it acceptable that: Values for leaf members and former non-leaf members may be duplicated, because they can later be filtered or removed using a cost classification flag. Attempted Approach I tried the following method: Use Model Copy (GUI) to copy data from the source model (leaf-level) Identify records where values are NULL (corresponding to former non-leaf members) Use an Advanced Formula step with: LINK() CONFIG.READ_CALCULATED_MEMBER_VALUES = ON to retrieve values from non-leaf members Issue Encountered Unlike GUI-based Model Copy: In Advanced Formula, it is not possible to leave source dimensions unassigned All source dimensions must be explicitly mapped or fixed to a specific member As a result, I cannot reproduce the same behavior as GUI Model Copy, which implicitly aggregates across unspecified dimensions. Questions Is there a way in SAC to physically populate data into members that were originally non-leaf, after converting them to leaf members? When a dimension has multiple hierarchies, is it possible to: Reference the original hierarchy (before leaf conversion) And use it to identify and assign values to members in the new leaf-based structure? Is there a recommended approach to replicate GUI-based Model Copy behavior (i.e., implicit aggregation across dimensions) using Advanced Formulas? Additional Context Preferably within the same model (no cross-model transformation unless necessary) The issue seems to stem from the difference between: GUI-based aggregation behavior Cell-based retrieval using LINK()

entry.title: Re: MT940 Consolidate Entry
entry.content:
Each :61: line in the file is a transaction in SAP.

Can you ask the bank to create a consolidated statement file?

Otherwise the contents of the file needs to be condensed within SAP (exit FEB0004) or by middleware.

entry.title: SAP EWM: коли стандартного управління складом уже недостатньо
entry.content:
Управління складом у більшості компаній починається з базових операцій: прийом, зберігання, відвантаження. Для цього вистачає стандартного SAP WM (Warehouse Management) або навіть паперового обліку. Але коли масштаб зростає, з'являється SAP Extended Warehouse Management - рішення для складів нового рівня.

Що таке SAP EWM і чим він відрізняється від WM

SAP WM - класичний модуль управління складом, вбудований у SAP ECC та S/4HANA. Він справляється з базовими задачами, але має обмеження: немає підтримки складних стратегій розміщення, обмежені можливості для автоматизації, немає інтеграції з роботизованими системами.

SAP EWM (Extended Warehouse Management) - це окрема, значно потужніша система для управління складською логістикою, яка може розгортатися як вбудований у S/4HANA або як автономне рішення.

Що вміє SAP EWM, чого не вміє WM

•       Складні стратегії путвеводителів і розміщення - система сама визначає оптимальну комірку для кожного товару

•       Управління ресурсами складу - планування навантаження комірників, балансування потоків

•       Labor Management - нормування праці, аналіз продуктивності персоналу

•       Yard Management - управління двором: черги вантажівок, доки, розклад

•       Інтеграція з автоматизацією - конвеєри, роботи, ASRS (автоматизовані системи зберігання)

•       RF-Framework - робота зі сканерами та мобільними пристроями в режимі реального часу

Вбудований EWM vs автономний EWM

З виходом S/4HANA 1709 SAP почав пропонувати EWM як вбудований компонент S/4HANA (Embedded EWM). Це спрощує інтеграцію та знижує вартість ліцензування. Автономний (Decentralized) EWM підходить для мереж складів, які обслуговують кілька ERP-систем одночасно.

💡 Практичне правило: якщо у вас один основний склад з відносно стандартними процесами - Embedded EWM у S/4HANA є оптимальним. Якщо мережа розподілених складів із різними власниками або системами - розгляньте Decentralized EWM.

Кому потрібен EWM?

•       Рітейлери та e-commerce з великими розподільчими центрами

•       Виробники зі складними процесами пікінгу та комплектації

•       Логістичні оператори (3PL), що обслуговують кількох клієнтів

•       Компанії, що планують або вже впровадили складську автоматизацію

Перехід з WM на EWM - значний проект, що вимагає ретельного планування процесів. Але компанії, що зробили цей крок, як правило, фіксують зростання продуктивності складу на 20-40% та суттєве скорочення помилок при відвантаженні.
#SAP #SAPEWM #WarehouseManagement #Logistics #WMS #SAPЦікавинки

entry.title: Re: Writing (Posting) Data to Non-Leaf Members in Advance...
entry.content:
I restructured my model based on previous advice by redefining the Account and Cost Center dimensions so that all members are leaf members, including those that were originally non-leaf members.
To preserve the original hierarchy logic, I stored the former non-leaf members as properties (keys) and used them to expand the # values for cost breakdown classification.

Current Behavior
After converting everything to leaf members:
Values are correctly assigned to members that were originally leaf members
However, members that were originally non-leaf do not have any data physically stored
In summary:
Former leaf members → values exist
Former non-leaf members → values do not exist

Requirement
I would like to understand:
How can I physically store fact data for members that were originally non-leaf?

Additional Assumption
For my use case, I consider it acceptable that:
Values for leaf members and former non-leaf members may be duplicated,
because they can later be filtered or removed using a cost classification flag.

Attempted Approach
I tried the following method:
Use Model Copy (GUI) to copy data from the source model (leaf-level)
Identify records where values are NULL (corresponding to former non-leaf members)
Use an Advanced Formula step with:
to retrieve values from non-leaf members
LINK()
CONFIG.READ_CALCULATED_MEMBER_VALUES = ON

Issue Encountered
Unlike GUI-based Model Copy:
In Advanced Formula, it is not possible to leave source dimensions unassigned
All source dimensions must be explicitly mapped or fixed to a specific member
As a result, I cannot reproduce the same behavior as GUI Model Copy,
which implicitly aggregates across unspecified dimensions.

Questions
Is there a way in SAC to physically populate data into members that were originally non-leaf, after converting them to leaf members?
When a dimension has multiple hierarchies, is it possible to:
Reference the original hierarchy (before leaf conversion)
And use it to identify and assign values to members in the new leaf-based structure?
Is there a recommended approach to replicate GUI-based Model Copy behavior
(i.e., implicit aggregation across dimensions) using Advanced Formulas?

Additional Context
Preferably within the same model (no cross-model transformation unless necessary)
The issue seems to stem from the difference between:
GUI-based aggregation behavior
Cell-based retrieval using LINK()

entry.title: ETM Compatibility Pack- How to proceed?
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, 

I am facing a situation where I am converting my SAP system from ECC to S/4HANA. In ECC we were using ETM module, but it is not available in S/4HANA. We are not using ETM.NEXT at the moment. We are trying to use compatibility pack for ETM: 119 ID. However, I have a few queries on how to proceed further?

I have implemented the sap note provided in the simplification list. But now, how to proceed. As there is no alternate solution present for ETM, will we have to connect both the ECC and S/4HANA systems?

If yes, how will data flow proceed (ECC to S/4HANA and S/4HANA to ECC) and how can we manage all the integration points between both the systems (master data and processes)?

Or can we use ETM in our S/4HANA environment alone? 

Can someone guide me, on how to proceed with further activities?

 

 

SAP ERP Central Component 

#S/4HANA

#Compatibilty

#Compatibility Packs

entry.title: Re: Unfair Result in C_S4CFI_2602 – System-Based Exam Com...
entry.content: Hello everyone, I was able to retake the certification and pass it. The technical issue was actually that I was using 2 screens, so even though I was completing the tasks correctly, the system was rejecting them and marking them as failed. So, if any of you are using a secondary monitor while taking the exam, I'd recommend not doing that anymore. As a precaution, I also made sure not to have any web pages or AI tools open — just an Excel file with my notes.

entry.title: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise Webinar Series - two sessions this week on 14th and 16th July
entry.content:
We have Two sessions coming up this week in the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series, both directly relevant for anyone making architectural decisions around AI. 
 
Session 7 - Monday 14 July | 17:00 CET / 11:00 ET / 08:00 PT An Architect's Guide to the SAP Family of Models With Markus Kohler, Simon Knehr , and Ashok Veilumuthu
SAP is the only company that has trained AI models on the full depth of its institutional knowledge — decades of business process expertise, application code, financial outcomes, and industry best practices. This session explores what the SAP Family of Models is, how business context delivers 3.5x higher prediction quality than generic LLMs, and what it means for architects evaluating AI model strategies. If you are deciding between generic foundation models and purpose-built enterprise models — or trying to explain the difference to your leadership — this is the session.
 
Session 8 - Wednesday 16 July | 14:00 CET / 08:00 ET / 05:00 PT -  The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to Reality
 
Every architect asks: what does the target architecture look like, and how do I get there? This session answers both - covering the AI-Native North Star Architecture (how AI, data, applications, and agents converge as a unified system), the AI Golden Path (a guided decision framework from idea to production) and the Architecture Center in General. Includes live demonstrations and worked examples showing how reference patterns translate into real implementation decisions. If you have been looking at the published assets on the SAP Architecture Center and wondering how to apply them, this is where it comes together.
Register once, attend any session: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

You can download and import the updated calendar file to ensure all remaining sessions are added to ...

 Slides and recordings from previous session here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/slides-amp-recordings-from-the-13-part-arc...

Feel free to share this with to anyone in your architecture, AI, or technology leadership teams who would benefit.

entry.title: Re: How to enforce mandatory Change Number for BOM change...
entry.content:
Dear @16175219,

I used the released BAdI Validate BOM Before Saving (BOM_BEFORE_SAVE) in the Custom Logic app. SAP provides this BAdI specifically for custom BOM validation before saving it can return an error but does not modify the BOM data itself

I tested the implementation in Maintain Bills of Material – Version 2 (F1813A)

Based on the described requirement, I prepared a proof of concept for a BOM item quantity change. Quantity was selected as a clear example of a BOM change that can be identified by comparing the value submitted for saving with the value currently stored for the same BOM item

Stored quantity

New quantity

Change Number on the changed item

Result

100

100

Empty or historical

No block because the quantity was not changed

100

101

Empty

Save is blocked

100

101

Assigned

Save is allowed

For example, when item 0010 is changed from quantity 100 to 101 using Change Number 2, the save is allowed. Another unchanged item can retain Change Number 1 or remain blank because it is not part of the current quantity change

The implementation uses the BOM item UUID to read the currently stored quantity from released CDS view I_MatlBillOfMaterialItemDEX. The CDS view provides detailed Material BOM item information that can be used for this comparison

DATA: ls_bom_item TYPE LINE OF if_bom_before_save=>tt_bom_item,

      ls_message LIKE LINE OF ct_message.

DATA: lv_saved_quantity LIKE ls_bom_item-billofmaterialitemquantity,

      lv_item_changed    TYPE abap_bool.

LOOP AT it_bom_item INTO ls_bom_item.

CLEAR:

    lv_saved_quantity,

    lv_item_changed.

lv_item_changed = abap_false.

"A new item does not yet have a persisted UUID

  IF ls_bom_item-billofmaterialitemuuid IS INITIAL.

lv_item_changed = abap_true.

ELSE.

"Read the currently saved quantity for this BOM item

    SELECT SINGLE BillOfMaterialItemQuantity

      FROM I_MatlBillOfMaterialItemDEX

      WHERE BillOfMaterialItemUUID =

            @ls_bom_item-billofmaterialitemuuid

      INTO @lv_saved_quantity.

    IF sy-subrc = 0.

      "Check whether the item quantity was changed

      IF ls_bom_item-billofmaterialitemquantity

         <> lv_saved_quantity.

        lv_item_changed = abap_true.

      ENDIF.

    ELSE.

      "No persisted item was found treat it as a new item

      lv_item_changed = abap_true.

    ENDIF.

  ENDIF.

  "Block a changed or new item when no Change Number is assigned

  IF lv_item_changed = abap_true

     AND ls_bom_item-engineeringchangedocument IS INITIAL.

    CLEAR ls_message.

    ls_message-msgty = 'E'.

    ls_message-msgid = '29'.

    ls_message-msgno = '045'.

    APPEND ls_message TO ct_message.

    EXIT.

  ENDIF.

ENDLOOP.

When the quantity is changed without a Change Number the BAdI returns:

29045 – Enter change number – BOM has history requirement

and prevents the BOM from being saved. This proof of concept confirms the technical feasibility of blocking a quantity change without a Change Number in F1813A

In the tested system, EngineeringChangeDocument is not exposed uniformly in every BAdI structure. Other scenarios such as component replacement, item deletion, changes to other item attributes or BOM header changes must therefore be evaluated and implemented separately, based on the fields available for the particular changed object

More details below:

SAP Help – BAdI: Validate BOM Before Saving

SAP Help – Maintain Bills of Material (Version 2)

SAP Help – Data Extraction for Material BOM Item

SAP KBA 3767381 – Mandatory Change Record Setting for Bill of Material Usage Does Not Block BOM Changes – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Teaching SAP Without Teaching SAP
entry.content:
Very interesting article Matt,

For e the reason I always thought the SAP University Alliances program stood out was because it enabled faculty at universities to teach concepts in an engaging way but also in an environment that students could expect to be comfortable with immediately upon graduating. One of the very first faculty to join the SAP University Alliances program in the UK was a Professor of logistics and supply chains that had formerly worked for a UK engineering firm called Lucas. He used an Excel sheet to teach logistics and lead time concepts. His biggest headache was that the Excel sheet didn't have an actual front-end to teach students how to access the information in a real-world way, but instead was restricted to teaching modelling only. When he moved over to SAP and then used SAC he continued to make models in Excel but he could also teach the students how to enter, clean, extract and interpret data. He also anted the students to use an industry standard as many of the students were leaning towards doing a Prince2 qualification and wanted an industry standard qualification to sit alongside.

For him SAP was a means to extend the student experience, at depth and rea-world applicability and then also add a prospective qualification route to enable the students to build a portfolio of experiences and qualifications around their subject knowledge.

So it was less about 'learning SAP' as much as it was understanding the systems that are used to manage the concepts that they were learning in their classes, and to give them a career boost by providing evidence for that understanding.

Martin

entry.title: Payment list layout vendor address block

Instead, only individual address columns are shown. Is this the standard design of RFZALI20, or is there a correction (SAP Note) or configuration that enables the formatted multi-line address output (like in RFZALI00) ?

entry.title: Re: Teaching Students to Work With an Unreliable but Inevitable Teammate: AI
entry.content:
Hi Matt,

I'm actually not as worried about AI usage as some. I've bene using ChatGPT to summarise my (occasionally wordy) texts for courses for a year or so and I've found it a valuable time saver - asking for bullet points, ten writing my own text, then asking ChatGPT to refine before going in and rewriting myself is rather lie having my own editor. What I haven't been doing is relying on it entirely and assuming everything it says is true. I've also been using Claude to create dashboards for me and it has done an excellent job - with the caveat that I once asked Claude to summarise a list of institutions by global university rankings and the rankings were (slightly) wrong.

For me there are two different ways to teach AI: 1) as a tool i.e. know the limitations and ensure you have intellectual control over the tool at all times - don't become a dependent; and b) as a business and IT transformational tool i.e. something you should be aware of the impact of. In this way it's rather like teaching students about how Excel (and Lotus 123 and Visicalc back in the day) work on the one hand, and how they impact on business and technical processes on the other.

Martin

entry.title: Supplier Payment Identification in Bank Statements for Consolidated APP Payments
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

We are currently processing supplier payments through the Automatic Payment Program (APP), where multiple supplier payments are grouped into a single payment batch and transmitted to the bank as a consolidated payment file. Consequently, the bank statement is received as a single batch entry rather than as individual payment transactions.

Our requirement is to understand whether SAP has any best-practice recommendations regarding the fields that should be populated for supplier payments in this scenario.

We have already reviewed the Reference and Payment Reference fields. However, these values are typically derived from the supplier invoice, and such information is not consistently available on all invoices. In addition, we are not planning to enable the Individual Payment option.

Ultimately, we are looking for a reliable identifier that can be passed through the payment process and returned on the bank statement, allowing us to determine which SAP payment document the bank statement entry relates to.

Any recommendations or best practices from SAP or from your implementation experience would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

Regards,
Naveen Kumar

entry.title: Re: Why we need to write more about our SAP Teaching Experiences
entry.content:
Hi Matteus,

I love the power of storytelling and equally recognise the power of a random conversation. Networking is crucial to both. A story is only as good as the teller and I find that a truly gifted storyteller can bring any subject to life - and by interacting with a wider network we learn more stories which then inform how we can teach, inspire and explain concepts in a fun, engaging and intellectually curious way.

Martin

entry.title: Re: Student Exchange Opportunities Among SAP Academic Partnership Universities
entry.content:
Hi Ariadne,

This is a really interesting idea. I know a great many students have the opportunity to study abroad even if there is not an SAP University Alliances exchange program per se. When I was at my second university I had the opportunity to either work in Toronto for a Canadian paper company with links to the University of Toronto, or do a student exchange program from my own school (the University of Manchester) to the Universite Catholique du Louvain. I chose Toronto though both were interesting options. My experience was invaluable as I had the opportunity to experience the major/minor system first hand, to experience how topics were taught from a North American perspective and also what North Americans thought of Europeans. It was a valuable experience educationally and culturally and it broadened my perspective on how big the world actually was. I have many fond memories.

I would suggest also brining this up at our next ACCs if you get the chance to attend. We could even include it in a future ACC as a discussion topic?

Martin

entry.title: Re: Budget Check Exception（Delivery date is empty） When A...
entry.content:
Good  day @YIDAN 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your descriptions, your main issue is not the budget availability check itself. The purchase requisition item is being saved with an invalid delivery or service period. Because of that, the system raises message 06633 and the PR item has no delivery date in EBAN-LFDAT.

In simple terms,

  • The service product SRV_01060 is checked against plant SH00 for the relevant delivery date or procurement period.
  • If your service end date or delivery date is missing or no longer valid after changing the quantity, the product listing or plant validity check cannot be completed correctly.
  • If you ignore the warning and you save the PR with an empty delivery date, the system may not create the commitment update for budget availability control as expected. Note that purchase requisition can consume budget through commitment updating. Also note that purchase requisition can impact the budget through predictive commitments management.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/93bd0b1e72ca4cbcbfb942d4497529f7/09a431d3fdcc4843914bbfba38686907.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bd39d476d1e34b48afed98759286efd6/a01315ea9245454aa85a2ec3389400b9.html?locale=en-US

Here are steps to consider:

Go to PR in Manage Purchase Requisition – Professional or the app you used to change the item. Then go to item 10 and check the service item dates. For PRs, EBAN-LFDAT represents the date on which the goods or service are delivered.

Ensure you check product SRV_01060 in Manage Product Master Data. You can use it to search, display, edit, create, and copy product master data. Ensure that the product is extended to plant SH00, not locked, and valid for purchasing or service procurement.

If SH00 is a retail site or listing relevant plant, ensure you check whether the product is listed or assigned for the required validity period. Assortments are a collection of products relevant for specific site such as stores and distribution centers.

You can return to PR, update the service period or delivery date, choose Check and then save again

Here are references for more guiding details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/0df90d3c7cb848eeb4a6832e96606c32.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/spmm/3362167296.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/0483875792f25c08e10000000a441470.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/64609d0ecac54654b0837cba34555b82/2476f2f2b2d749dea7a96664ac391d50.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June
entry.content:
Hi @Martin_Kuma,

good question and thanks for the detailed test.

The setting works as a global on/off switch, not as a self-service subscription. It controls whether you receive emails from Datasphere at all. But you still need to be listed as a recipient in the individual task chain or replication flow configuration. That part hasn't changed.

Your expectation, receiving failure notifications without being in the recipient list, is not in scope for this feature. For that you'd need a separate "subscribe to all failures" capability. Worth raising on SAP Influence if that's what you're looking for.

Best
KP

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud Architecture: Datasphere, Databricks, and the Unified Data Layer
entry.content:
This blog covers the technical architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) — focusing on the integration between SAP Datasphere, Databricks, and SAP Analytics Cloud. It is written for data architects and technical leads designing data platform strategy on a modern SAP landscape.

Applies to: SAP Business Data Cloud (GA as of 2024). SAP Datasphere (all current releases). SAP Analytics Cloud. Databricks (Unity Catalog enabled). This article references the jointly engineered SAP-Databricks integration — not the legacy SAP HANA Databricks connector.

1. BDC architectural layers — how they connect

SAP Business Data Cloud operates across three interconnected layers. Understanding the technical responsibility of each layer is essential for architecture planning:

 
LayerComponentPrimary FunctionKey Objects
Semantic & GovernanceSAP DatasphereBusiness meaning, data federation, access controlSpaces, Business Layer, Data Access Controls, Replication Flows
Compute & MLDatabricks (Unity Catalog)Lakehouse storage, ML model training, advanced analyticsDelta Lake tables, ML Experiments, Feature Store, Unity Catalog
Analytical UXSAP Analytics CloudBI, planning, predictive analyticsStories, Models, Analytic Applications, Planning Models
Business ContentSAP Content PackagesPre-built data products for business domainsFinance Data Product, Supply Chain Data Product, HR Data Product
 

2. Datasphere configuration for BDC

Datasphere acts as the semantic and governance layer in BDC. Before connecting to Databricks, Datasphere must be configured with the correct Space structure and replication flows from SAP source systems.

Space architecture for BDC

Design one Datasphere Space per business domain. Each Space is a governed, isolated data workspace with its own access controls:

Recommended Datasphere Space structure for BDC

Datasphere Tenant ├─ Space: FIN (Finance domain) │ ├─ Replication Flow: S/4HANA Finance → FIN (via SAP HANA Cloud or ODP) │ ├─ Data Layer: ACDOCA, BSEG, FAGLFLEXT (raw replication) │ ├─ Business Layer: Finance Fact View, Cost Centre Dimension, Profit Centre Dimension │ └─ Data Access Control: Finance_Users (row-level by Company Code) ├─ Space: SCM (Supply Chain) │ └─ (same pattern: replicate → model → govern) └─ Space: HR └─ (additional GDPR Data Access Controls for personal data)

Connecting Datasphere to Databricks

The BDC integration uses a jointly engineered connector between Datasphere and Databricks Unity Catalog. Configuration steps:

1. Create a Databricks connection in Datasphere

In Datasphere → Connections → New Connection → Databricks. Provide your Databricks workspace URL, personal access token (or service principal credentials), and HTTP path for the SQL warehouse.

2. Configure Unity Catalog metastore sharing

In Databricks Unity Catalog, create a catalog for SAP data (e.g. sap_business_data). Grant the Datasphere service principal USE CATALOG and CREATE TABLE privileges on this catalog.

3. Create data flows from Datasphere to Databricks

In Datasphere → Data Integration → Data Flows. Create a flow from your Business Layer view to the Databricks target catalog. Schedule for incremental replication using watermark-based CDC.

4. Validate semantic preservation in Databricks

Query the replicated table in Databricks. Verify that business-meaningful column names (not SAP technical field names like BUKRS) and calculated measures are preserved in the Delta table schema.

3. SAP Content Packages — pre-built data products

SAP provides pre-built business content packages for BDC that encode SAP's domain knowledge into governed data products. These reduce the time-to-value for common analytical use cases:

Content PackageSource SystemsKey Measures IncludedAI Use Cases Enabled
Finance Data ProductS/4HANA (FI/CO)Revenue, Cost, EBITDA, Cash Flow by dimensionFinancial forecasting, anomaly detection, close acceleration
Supply Chain Data ProductS/4HANA (MM/SD/PP)On-time delivery, inventory turns, OTIF, lead timeDemand forecasting, supply risk scoring, stock optimisation
HR Data ProductSuccessFactors, S/4HANA HRHeadcount, attrition rate, time-to-hire, engagementAttrition prediction, workforce planning, skills gap analysis
Procurement Data ProductS/4HANA (MM), AribaSpend by category, supplier performance, PO cycle timeSupplier risk scoring, spend analytics, contract compliance

4. ML model development on BDC data

With SAP business data available in Databricks via the BDC integration, ML models can be trained directly on semantically-rich, governed data:

 
Example: Demand forecasting model on BDC Supply Chain data (Python / MLflow)

import mlflow import pandas as pd from databricks.sdk.runtime import spark from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor # Read from BDC Supply Chain data product in Unity Catalog df = spark.table("sap_business_data.supply_chain.sales_order_history").toPandas() # Features derived from SAP semantic layer (business-meaningful names preserved) features = ['material_group', 'sales_org', 'distribution_channel', 'customer_segment', 'month_of_year', 'historical_demand_lag_4w'] target = 'confirmed_quantity' X = df[features] y = df[target] with mlflow.start_run(run_name="demand-forecast-gbr-v1"): model = GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=200, max_depth=5) model.fit(X_train, y_train) mlflow.log_metric("rmse", rmse) mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, "demand_forecast_model", registered_model_name="sap_demand_forecast")

Key advantage of BDC data: The material_group, sales_org, and customer_segment columns retain their SAP business meaning from the Datasphere semantic layer. Without BDC, these would typically arrive as cryptic SAP technical codes (e.g. MATKL, VKORG) requiring additional transformation before use in ML feature engineering.

5. Data quality prerequisite checklist

BDC amplifies your SAP data — quality problems at source become quality problems in the lakehouse and in AI outputs. Before adopting BDC, validate:

  • Material master: classification completeness > 95%, no duplicate materials (check with MDG deduplication report)
  • Customer master: address standardization complete, duplicate business partner rate < 2%
  • Financial postings: period-end closing rates consistently > 98%, no large volumes of parked documents
  • Historical data depth: minimum 24 months of transactional data for meaningful ML training

Summary

  • BDC operates across three layers: Datasphere (semantic/governance), Databricks (compute/ML), SAP Analytics Cloud (analytical UX)
  • Design one Datasphere Space per business domain with its own replication flows, business layer, and data access controls
  • The BDC-Databricks connector preserves SAP business-meaningful column names in Delta Lake — eliminating the need for semantic reconstruction in the lakehouse
  • SAP Content Packages provide pre-built, governed data products for Finance, Supply Chain, HR, and Procurement
  • Validate MDG data quality before adopting BDC — set quantitative thresholds for completeness and uniqueness

Have you implemented Datasphere or are you evaluating SAP Business Data Cloud? I'd welcome technical questions and experiences in the comments — particularly around the Databricks connector configuration and content package adoption.

entry.title: RISE with SAP: Technical Architecture Decisions Every Architect Must Make Before Go-Live
entry.content:
This blog focuses on the technical and architectural decisions specific to RISE with SAP implementations — the choices that need to be made before the program starts, not discovered during it. It complements SAP's official RISE methodology documentation with practitioner-level detail on the decisions that most frequently require course-correction mid-program.

Applies to: RISE with SAP (all editions). S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition and Public Edition. SAP BTP Integration Suite for RISE connectivity. SAP Activate methodology for RISE implementations. Relevant for both greenfield and brownfield (system conversion) approaches.

1. Private Edition vs Public Edition: the architecture consequences

This is the most consequential technical decision in a RISE programme. The editions differ in fundamental ways that affect the entire implementation approach:

DimensionS/4HANA Cloud, Private EditionS/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition
Upgrade cadenceCustomer-controlled (typically 1–2× per year)SAP-controlled (quarterly, non-negotiable)
Customisation modelBAdIs, customer exits, some modificationsBAdIs and released APIs only (strict clean core)
Transport managementCustomer-managed CTS / gCTSSAP-managed (no custom transports)
Industry solutionsFull industry solution supportSelected industries (check current availability)
Data migration toolingLTMC, LTMOM, custom migration objectsMigration Cockpit (pre-configured object types only)
Integration approachBTP Integration Suite (recommended), direct RFCs still possibleBTP Integration Suite mandatory for all external integrations

Architecture decision point: If your organization requires industry-specific SAP functionality (e.g. SAP IS-U for utilities, SAP for Banking, SAP for Retail with specific localization requirements), validate that functionality availability in Public Edition before committing. Some capabilities are Private Edition only. This validation must happen in the Explore phase of SAP Activate, not after contract signature.

2. BTP architecture for RISE — what's included and what you configure

RISE includes BTP entitlements, but "included" does not mean "configured." The BTP services bundled with RISE require deliberate architectural planning:

Integration Suite — RISE connectivity patterns

All external integrations to S/4HANA Cloud (in both editions) should route through BTP Integration Suite. SAP provides pre-built integration content via the SAP Integration Content Catalog for common integration scenarios (SAP ↔ Salesforce, SAP ↔ Workday, SAP ↔ logistics providers).

1. Activate Integration Suite in your RISE BTP subaccount

Navigate to BTP Cockpit → Subscriptions → SAP Integration Suite. Activate the standard plan (included in RISE entitlements).

2. Download pre-built integration packages from the catalog

In Integration Suite → Discover → Content Catalog. Search for your integration scenario (e.g. "S/4HANA Cloud Sales Order to Salesforce"). Import the relevant package.

3. Configure the communication arrangement in S/4HANA

In S/4HANA Cloud, create a Communication Arrangement for the relevant Communication Scenario. This defines the inbound/outbound API endpoints that Integration Suite will use.

4. Configure credentials and deploy

Add communication system credentials in Integration Suite Security Material. Deploy the integration flow and activate monitoring via the Operations Monitor.

3. Clean core assessment and extension migration planning

Every ABAP custom object in your current SAP ECC or S/4HANA on-premise landscape requires a disposition decision for RISE. SAP provides tooling to support this assessment:

Custom Code Migration tool (CCMT)

The Custom Code Migration Tool (transaction CCMT in the source system, or available via SAP Cloud ALM) analyses your custom ABAP objects and classifies them:

CCMT classification categories

GREEN — Compatible with S/4HANA Cloud, no changes required YELLOW — Requires adaptation (uses deprecated APIs or syntax) RED — Incompatible, must be redesigned or retired GREY — Not relevant (dead code, unused objects)

For RISE implementations, RED objects require the most strategic attention: each one is a decision about whether to redesign as a BTP extension, retire the business requirement, or accept as a gap and adjust the process to the standard.

Recommended approach: Run CCMT at least 3 months before the start of the Realise phase. Feed the results into your Clean Core Backlog — a structured list of every non-standard object with its disposition decision, owner, estimated effort, and target (BTP extension / retire / accept standard). Review this backlog weekly during Realise.

4. Data migration architecture for RISE

Data migration is the most technically complex and most underestimated workload in RISE implementations. The tooling differs between editions:

Migration Cockpit (Public Edition primary tool)

SAP Migration Cockpit in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides file-based migration for a defined set of object types. Key constraints:

  • Object types are pre-defined by SAP — custom migration objects are not supported
  • Migration templates are provided as Excel files — data preparation must conform exactly to the template structure
  • Staging area in S/4HANA Cloud — no direct database access
  • Simulation mode allows multiple test migrations before production cutover

Critical constraint: If your ECC landscape contains custom Z-tables with business data that has no equivalent standard object type in Migration Cockpit, you will need an alternative migration strategy (BTP-based migration application or manual data entry). Identify these scenarios during the Explore phase — they significantly affect the cutover plan.

5. SAP Cloud ALM for RISE programme management

SAP Cloud ALM is included with RISE and provides project management, test management, and operations monitoring capabilities designed specifically for SAP cloud transformations. Key capabilities for RISE architects:

  • Fit-to-Standard Analysis — digital workshop tool for capturing fit/gap decisions during the Explore phase
  • Process Management — business process hierarchy aligned to SAP Best Practices, with delta documentation for custom processes
  • Test Management — test case management integrated with SAP's pre-built test scripts for standard processes
  • Integration Monitoring — real-time visibility into Integration Suite message processing, error rates, and performance

Summary

  • Private vs Public Edition is the most consequential RISE architecture decision — validate industry solution availability before committing
  • All external integrations should route through BTP Integration Suite — pre-built catalog content is available for common scenarios
  • Run CCMT 3+ months before Realise phase — feed results into a Clean Core Backlog with weekly review
  • Migration Cockpit (Public Edition) supports pre-defined object types only — identify custom Z-table migration scenarios early
  • SAP Cloud ALM is included with RISE — use Fit-to-Standard Analysis and Test Management from project start

Have you navigated the Private vs Public Edition decision in a RISE program? Or run a CCMT assessment and had to make difficult clean core disposition decisions? I'd welcome your experience in the comments — these are the decisions where community knowledge is most valuable.

entry.title: Building an Enterprise AI Architecture on SAP: Joule, BTP AI Core, and the Data Foundation
entry.content:
This blog covers the architectural layers required for enterprise-grade AI in SAP landscapes — from the data foundation through AI services to governance. It is intended for solution architects, enterprise architects, and technical leads designing AI capability on SAP BTP.

Applies to: SAP Joule (embedded across SAP cloud applications as of 2024/2025), SAP BTP AI Core (all available plans), SAP AI Launchpad, SAP Datasphere, SAP Business Data Cloud. EU AI Act considerations apply to deployments within the European Economic Area.

1. Joule: capabilities, technical architecture, and integration points

Joule is built on SAP's AI Foundation — a layer within BTP that provides the infrastructure for SAP's own AI features and for customer-built AI applications. At a technical level, Joule integrates with:

  • S/4HANA Cloud — via OData APIs and SAP Fiori launchpad integration for natural language ERP interactions
  • SuccessFactors — embedded in Recruiting, Performance & Goals, and Compensation modules
  • SAP Service Cloud — case intelligence, next-best-action recommendations
  • SAP Ariba — sourcing guidance and contract intelligence

Joule activation prerequisites

1. SAP AI Core subscription in your BTP subaccount

Joule depends on SAP AI Core. Ensure the aicore service plan is subscribed in your BTP Global Account entitlements.

2. Trust configuration between BTP and your SAP cloud applications

Joule's cross-application context requires correctly configured identity propagation. Verify your IAS (Identity Authentication Service) tenant is connected to both BTP and your SAP cloud application tenants.

3. Data privacy and residency validation

Confirm that your data processing agreement (DPA) covers Joule's AI model invocations. For EU deployments, verify data residency settings in the SAP AI Core configuration.

4. Role assignment

Assign the Joule_User role collection to the relevant user groups in your BTP subaccount. Business users require no additional technical configuration after role assignment.

2. BTP AI Core: custom model deployment architecture

SAP BTP AI Core aicore provides the managed infrastructure for deploying, serving, and monitoring custom ML models. It uses a resource group model to isolate workloads:

 
BTP AI Core resource group structure

AI Core Instance ├─ Resource Group: finance-forecasting │ ├─ Scenario: demand-forecast-v1 │ ├─ Configuration: model hyperparameters, data connections │ ├─ Training Pipeline: connected to Datasphere dataset │ └─ Deployment: REST endpoint for inference ├─ Resource Group: hr-attrition │ └─ (isolated from finance workloads) └─ Resource Group: supply-chain-anomaly └─ (isolated from all other workloads)

LLM integration via Generative AI Hub

SAP AI Core's Generative AI Hub provides governed access to foundation models from multiple providers — including Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o), Anthropic (Claude), Google (Gemini), and Meta (Llama) — through a single, governed endpoint:

 
Generative AI Hub — API call pattern (Python SDK)

from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub') llm = ChatOpenAI( proxy_model_name='gpt-4o', proxy_client=proxy_client, max_tokens=1000 ) response = llm.invoke("Summarise the top 5 overdue purchase orders for supplier X")

Data governance note: All prompts sent through Generative AI Hub are governed by your SAP AI Core data processing agreement. Review your DPA before sending personally identifiable information (PII) or commercially sensitive data in prompts. Use data masking or anonymisation where required by your data classification policy.

3. The data foundation: Datasphere, MDG, and BDC

The quality and governance of the data layer determines AI output quality more than the choice of model. Three components form the foundation:

SAP Datasphere — semantic modelling

Datasphere's semantic layer preserves business meaning as data moves from SAP source systems to analytical and AI consumers. Key configuration elements:

  • Spaces — isolated data workspaces per business domain (Finance Space, Supply Chain Space). Each Space has its own data access controls, replication flows, and analytical models.
  • Business Layer objects — Dimensions, Facts, and Analytical Datasets that expose data with business-meaningful column names and relationships
  • Data Access Controls — row-level security applied at the semantic layer, ensuring AI queries respect the same access controls as human users

Master Data Governance (MDG)

AI models trained on or querying data with poor master data quality will produce unreliable outputs. Validate these before any AI initiative:


MDG DomainAI relevanceKey quality check
Customer (BP)Customer segmentation, churn predictionDuplicate rate, address completeness
MaterialDemand forecasting, anomaly detectionClassification consistency, UoM standardisation
Finance (GL)Financial forecasting, anomaly detectionCost centre hierarchy completeness
SupplierRisk scoring, payment predictionBank data completeness, country standardisation

 4. EU AI Act readiness for SAP AI deployments

For deployments within the EEA, the EU AI Act (effective August 2024, obligations phasing in through 2026) introduces requirements for AI systems that interact with business data. Key considerations for SAP AI architectures:

  • Risk classification: Classify each AI use case as Minimal, Limited, High, or Unacceptable risk under the EU AI Act taxonomy. Most SAP operational AI use cases (demand forecasting, anomaly detection) fall under Limited or High risk.
  • High-risk requirements: Human oversight mechanisms, model documentation (technical files), conformity assessment, and registration in the EU AI database
  • Transparency: Users must be informed when they are interacting with an AI system (Joule's disclosure mechanism satisfies this for embedded co-pilot interactions)
  • Data governance: Training data must be documented, bias-checked, and governed — Datasphere's lineage and audit capabilities support this requirement

Summary

  • Joule requires AI Core subscription + IAS trust configuration + DPA validation before activation
  • Use Resource Groups in AI Core to isolate AI workloads by domain
  • Generative AI Hub provides governed multi-model LLM access — review DPA before sending sensitive data
  • Datasphere Spaces + Business Layer objects are the semantic foundation for AI data quality
  • Validate MDG quality in each domain before building AI use cases on that domain's data
  • EU AI Act obligations are phasing in through 2026 — classify your AI use cases now

What AI use cases are you building on SAP BTP? I'm particularly interested in hearing from practitioners who have gone through the Generative AI Hub integration or built custom models on AI Core — what were your biggest technical challenges?

entry.title: Master SAP Autonomous Finance
entry.content:
Be the Finance Strategist Your Clients Count On!

If you’re the consultant colleagues call when strategy meets execution, this eLearning is for you. “Understanding the Autonomous Enterprise” and Autonomous Finance” show how SAP’s AI-first architecture turns fragmented finance into a connected, real-time, outcomes-driven operation. This course addresses you, if you are a Finance consultant or an Enterprise Architect, a Value Advisor or a presales expert who shapes shaping AI roadmaps.

Start this free course now: Exploring Autonomous Finance.

Why this course matters now

Finance is at an inflection point. Manual reconciliations, periodic closes, and siloed reports can’t keep pace with volatility. SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise flips the script: assistants and agents manage end-to-end processes while humans stay in control at the moments that matter - compressing close cycles, improving forecasts, strengthening cash and compliance, and freeing teams for higher-value work.

Dive deep into Autonomous Finance

See how finance evolves from periodic reporting to continuous intelligence across Revenue Management, Accounting & Close, FP&A, Treasury & Working Capital, Tax & Trade, and GRC. Explore role-aligned assistants that coordinate agents to get work done:

  • Billing, AR/AP, and Sales Ops Assistants to accelerate cash and reduce errors
  • Financial Closing and Consolidation Assistants to shorten cycles with higher accuracy
  • Financial Planning Assistant for always-on forecasting and scenario analysis
  • Cash & Treasury Assistant for real-time visibility and resilient liquidity
  • Tax & Compliance and Governance Assistants for continuous compliance and audit readiness

All with human-in-the-loop by default, grounded in SAP’s encoded process expertise and trusted data fabric.

What you’ll walk away with

  • A clear blueprint for Autonomous Finance and how it plugs into the broader Autonomous Enterprise
  • Concrete use cases and measurable value levers (faster closes, better forecasts, stronger cash, continuous compliance)
  • Practical guidance to start domain-by-domain and scale with a consistent, governed architecture

Ready to lead the shift from reporting the past to shaping what happens next?

Enroll now and become the go‑to architect of Autonomous Finance - delivering leaner, faster, and more resilient outcomes for your customers.

entry.title: Start remote Process/Actions in BTP ABAP via Task Chains from SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Modern cloud architectures allow for versatile and open interoperability. So does SAP Datasphere and SAP BTP ABAP, too. This example outlines some essentials how Datasphere can kick-off actions defined on the ABAP Cloud side, beyond system boundaries.

Starting a Task/Process in BTP ABAP remotely from SAP Datasphere using Task Chains opens up new scenarios for interoperability and integration. The goal is to orchestrate remote tasks initiated by SAP Datasphere — securely, programmatically, and fully automated.

We will cover the below points:

  • Creating a Task Chain in Datasphere including an API Call to ABAP Cloud
  • Connection in Datasphere to ABAP Cloud (for direct API call)
  • Setting up Communication System & Arrangement in ABAP Cloud
  • Executing the Task Chain in Datasphere: Trigger actions in ABAP Cloud

 

1 Architecture Overview

Architecture

The SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Task: Datasphere acts as the caller. ABAP Cloud exposes an endpoint (inbound Web Api) to trigger an action.

Option (2) and (3) complete the picture of possible solution approaches. Main differences as well as pros & cons are outlined further down in one of the last sections.

The Solution Architecture displays 3 different options:

  • (1) Use Direct API Integration 
    • Straightforward
    • Minimal cost and complexity
    • Perfect for nightly batch orchestration, event-driven data loads, or simple API-driven analytical data refresh
  • (2) Integration Suite
    • Integration involves complex message transformations, protocol mediation, multiple target systems, or when centralized API governance and monitoring across the enterprise are required.
    • Especially appropriate where iFlow reusability justifies the additional cost and setup effort or when integration suite is already in place and a common tool.
  • (3) CAP Application
    • Custom application logic is needed beyond what a direct API call/iFlow can provide
    • E.g. multi-source data aggregation, advanced parsing, enrichments
    • Additional persistence required
    • Well-suited for reusable microservice-style integrations on BTP

 

2 Prerequisites

You need

  1. SAP Datasphere tenant with developer + admin access
  2. Task Chain to design the flow of events
  3. SAP BTP ABAP Environment (ABAP Cloud) with developer access
  4. BTP ABAP: Authorization for OAuth clients, Communication Arrangements, ABAP development of Web APIs

 

 3 Creating Communication Artifacts in ABAP Cloud

Navigation: ABAP Environment Web Access → Communication Management → Communication Systems/Arrangements

Communication Arrangement

 

Communication Arrangement

Communication System

Communication System 1Communication System 2

Communication/Connection User:

Create a User for Inbound Communication and use the same in the subsequent step on Datasphere level for the creation of a generic HTTP connection.

 

4 ABAP Cloud Artifacts

For the sake of simplicity I won't go into the details of all of the development artifacts and configuration settings.

For sure, you will create:

  • Inbound Service
  • Service Binding
  • Communication Scenario BTP ABAP
  • Service Definition
  • Entity
  • Class implementation

An example of an action definition processData (ZI_DEMO_SERVICE):

managed implementation in class zbp_i_demo_service unique;

define behavior for ZI_DEMO_SERVICE alias DemoService
persistent table zdemoservice
lock master
{
  field ( readonly ) Id;
  field ( numbering : managed ) Id;

  create;
  update;
  delete;

  action processData result [1] zdemoservice;

  mapping for zdemoservice
  {
    Id = id;
    Name = name;
    Description = description;
    Status = status;
    CreatedBy = created_by;
    CreatedAt = created_at;
    LastChangedBy = last_changed_by;
    LastChangedAt = last_changed_at;
    LocalLastChanged = local_last_changed;
  }
}

An example of an ABAP Class with an action implementation:

CLASS lhc_DemoService DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
 
    PRIVATE SECTION.
 
    METHODS processData FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION DemoService~processData result result.
 
ENDCLASS.
 
CLASS lhc_DemoService IMPLEMENTATION.
  
  METHOD processData.
    " Your business logic implementation her
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Service Definition:

@EndUserText.label: 'Demo Service Definition'
define service ZUI_DEMO_SERVICE {
  expose ZC_DEMO_SERVICE as DemoService;
}

Inbound Service:

Communication Scenario:

 

5 Create a Connection

The first step is creating a generic HTTP connection to the endpoint exposed by ABAP Cloud. In order to do so, consider the following details. Take the connection host from the BTP ABAP instance key. The credentials must be taken from the connection user as defined in the communication arrangement.

 

An alternative to Username/Password is an OAuth based authentication mechanism. 

 

6 Creating a Task Chain in SAP Datasphere

The first step is defining a Task Chain that encapsulates the processing logic you want to execute .

In the given example:

  • The Task Chain starts with a API Task
  • Sends an email in case of success

Task Chain

In the API Task details, define the following:

  • Method POST
  • API Path - the path to the service endpoint of your ABAP Web API that you want to trigger
  • Mode Synchronous

API Task Details 1

 

Make sure to fetch the CSRF Token. The URL which I've used is simply the $metadata path of the service endpoint:

https://<ABAP_HOST>.abap.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZUI_DEMO_SERVICE_O4/$metadata

 

7 Execution Results

The Task Chain in Datasphere successfully executed the ABAP Web API call:

Task Chain API successfully executed

For monitoring and troubleshooting purpose, individual API calls can be traced from BTP ABAP side. The HTTP trace can be turned on for granular levels.

 

8 Solution Options - Comparisons

The following comparison outlines the key aspects per solution/architecture option as depicted in the initial architecture diagram. I have prototyped all of the below and all options are working flawlessly. Especially w.r.t. to the CAP based solution, there is a wide open room to play with.

 

Option

Pros (+)

Cons (-)

(1) Direct API

  • Simplest setup & fewest components & lowest cost
  • No middleware latency
  • Lightweight and developer-friendly
  • Limited transformation capability
  • Less suitable for multi-step or conditional routing logic

(2) Integration Suite

  • Supports complex transformations, multiple protocols and error handling in iFlows
  • Centralized API governance via API Management
  • Recommended for complex mediated flows
  • Two-step connectivity required (Integration Suite + Datasphere)
  • Additional licensing cost
  • Higher setup and maintenance effort

(3) CAP CF App

  • Maximum flexibility for custom logic (parsing, filtering, enrichment, retry)
  • CAP integrates natively with HANA Cloud/Datasphere HDI
  • Supports secure credential management
  • Reusable microservice pattern
  • Requires application development and BTP CF deployment skills
  • Higher operational overhead
  • More BTP resource consumption
  • CAP app lifecycle must be managed independently

 

9 Conclusion/Outlook & Further aspects:

Overview of the major building blocks:

Capability

Result

No hardcoded secrets

via Communication Arrangements/System

Remote orchestration

Creating an end-to-end orchestrated chain of tasks, bidirectional if required

Cloud-native ABAP

Using appropriate ABAP Class APIs

Datasphere automation

Using Task Chain API

 

Use Cases that the implementation enables:

  • Nightly batch orchestration
  • Event-driven data loads
  • Cross-system workflows (this was our ultimate focus)
  • API-driven (transactional/analytical) data refresh

Limitations:

  • The Task Chain/API Task can't be parameterized dynamically with runtime parameters to e.g. initiate calls that have a variable payload in header or body
  • Moreover, the write-back/storing of HTTP responses is limited too

References:

  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Documentation:  https://api.sap.com/api/DatasphereTasks/overview
  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain Help Page: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/c8a54ee704e94e15926551293243fd1d/274f2736465c4c48a091c67588...

This blog entry complements my other blog on Triggering SAP Datasphere/BDC Task Chains from SAP BTP ABAP Cloud using the Task Chain REST API.

In case of questions feel free to raise them and please leave a comment if you found something incorrect - thanks for any help on that. I'm hoping this blog can support somebody out there and may facilitate some problem solving.

entry.title: Using Org Fields to Define Your SmartRecruiters System Logic
entry.content:
This post reviews the nine key areas within SmartRecruiters where Org Fields can be used to define and differentiate system logic, giving you both the flexibility to adapt to local or functional requirements and the governance to maintain consistency at scale.

 

What are Org Fields?

In the context of SmartRecruiters, org fields are used to distinguish between different recruiting processes. They allow us to build out approval workflows based on different conditions, determine applicable fields and assign the relevant hiring process and selection criteria.

Despite the name, org fields in SmartRecruiters do not necessarily need to correlate with the organizational structure configured in SuccessFactors Employee Central (EC). While there is a certain level of overlap, anything used to create a condition in SmartRecruiters has to be an set as an org field.

While standard org fields are available by default in SmartRecruiters, additional custom org fields can be defined as needed (please note current system limitation of 22 custom Org fields per instance which once created, cannot be deleted).    

 

Where are org fields used?

1  Job Approval Workflow

Org Fields determine which approval process (if any) is triggered when a new job is created. This makes it possible to enforce rigorous oversight when needed while keeping the approval process simple for other jobs.

Example:  Approval Chain determined by LEVEL OF EXPERIENCE

›  Executive-level jobs → complex, multi-step approval process

›  Graduate-level jobs → shortened or no approval required

Configuring an approval chain scoped to specific Org Field values in SmartRecruiters

 

2  Applicant Experience

Org Fields determine the information requested in the candidate-facing application form including which easy apply methods are available, which standard will fields appear and whether certain inputs - such as a CV or education history - are required or optional.

Example:  Applicant Experience determined by JOB TYPE

›  High-volume jobs → simplified form with limited fields; CV not mandatory

›  Specialist jobs → CV mandatory, parsing enabled, education fields required

Assigning Org Fields to application experience to control which jobs it applies to.

 

3  Application Fields

Beyond the initial application form, Org Fields determine which supplementary data fields are added to a candidate's profile, offer form and/or new hire form. This data may be collected at various stages of the recruitment process.

Example:  Application fields determined by COUNTRY

›  Jobs in the USA → fields include Veteran Status and Social Security Number

›  Jobs in South Africa → fields include Gender and Ethnicity

Creating a custom application field scoped to specific org fields.

 

4  Screening Questions

Org Fields determine which screening question set is presented to candidates during initial application. This enables targeted pre-qualification aligned to the specific requirements of a role, without exposing irrelevant questions to candidates for whom they are not applicable.

Example:  Screening Questions determined by LEVEL OF EXPERIENCE

›  Graduate-level roles:

–  What year are you graduating?

–  What is your major?

›  Management-level roles:

–  How many years of relevant management experience do you have?

–  Do you hold a valid passport?

A question set configured to apply only to APAC job postings via Org Field country values.

 

5  Hiring Process

The stages that make up your hiring process can be tailored according to Org Field/s. Whether driven by regional labor regulations, operational preferences, or role complexity, Org Fields ensure each job follows the appropriate process.

Example:  Hiring Process determined by COUNTRY

›  Egypt → streamlined process: 1 screening stage, 1 interview stage, 2 offer stages

›  Germany → complex process: 2 pre-screening stages, 3 interview stages, 1 assessment stage, 5 offer stages

A hiring process configured for North America with stages scoped by country Org Field.

 

6  Communication Templates

Automated and manual communications can be governed by Org Fields, allowing you to align messaging to the relevant brand, audience segment, etc.

Example:  Communication Templates determined by BRAND and LEVEL OF EXPERIENCE

›  Executive jobs under Brand A → formal emails with Brand A colors and logo

›  Graduate jobs under Brand B → informal emails with Brand B colors and logo

Scoping a communication template to specific brands, countries and departments via Org Fields.

 

7  Offer Approvals

Just as with job approvals, Org Fields define the approval flow for the offer stage. This allows organisations to apply stronger controls where compensation or role criticality demands it, while keeping the process efficient for roles where speed is a competitive advantage.

Example:  Approval Process determined by JOB TYPE

›  Specialist jobs → more complex offer approval chain

›  High-volume jobs → shorter or no approvals required

An offer approval chain scoped to specific org fields.

 

8  Offer Letter Templates

The offer letter presented to a successful candidate can be dynamically selected based on Org Fields. This supports differentiated templates for seniority levels, employment types, or geographies; ensuring the right legal language and formatting is applied.

Example:  Offer Letter determined by LEVEL OF EXPERIENCE and TYPE OF EMPLOYMENT

›  Graduate level, temporary employment → dedicated offer letter template tailored for early-career hires

›  Executive level, permanent employment → dedicated offer letter template tailored for senior appointments

An offer letter template scoped to a specific country.

 

9  Access Groups

Access Groups represent a dynamic permission layer applied on top of a system role. By tying Access Groups to specific Org Fields, you create a targeted level of control over what users can see and do, restricting visibility and actions to only the jobs and data relevant to each user's scope.

Example:  Permissions based on JOB FUNCTION and LOCATION

›  Role A → access restricted to Sales jobs in France only

›  Role B → access restricted to IT jobs in North America only

Example:  Permissions based on BRAND

›  Role C → access restricted to jobs associated with Brand X only 

An access group limiting visibility to North America, configured via country Org Field values.

  

Summary

Org Fields are far more than a configuration option, they are the backbone of a contextually aware recruiting system. When thoughtfully designed, they allow a single SmartRecruiters instance to serve the needs of a globally diverse organisation: adapting approvals, experiences, communications, and permissions to the precise context of each job, without sacrificing oversight or consistency.

Whether you are managing multi-brand environments, navigating country-specific compliance, or simply trying to reduce friction for high-volume hiring, Org Fields provide the structural foundation to make your system work the way your organisation does.
